Regulation 18901(a) .
In addition, Regulation 18901 provides a series of Iiurited
exceptions to the prohibition. 
For examPle, Regutation - -
1890i(b) (9) (A) (1) lrovides that a urass rnail'ing of the following is
not prohibited bY Section 89001:
An announcenent sent to an elected officer's
constituents concerning a public neetinq which is
airectfy related to the elected officer's 
incurnbent
governrnental duties, which is to be held by the
elected officer, and which the elected officer
intends to attend. Il]
( Ernphas is added. )
The purpose of the prohibition in Section 89001 was to
elininate thl potential, unfair advantage which rnass nailings
featuring an i-ncumbent night provide to an incumbent during the
eLection period. 
(Section 81002(e).) The basis for the
exceptioni in negulation 18901(b) was to ensure that officials
woul-d be able to carry out the duties associated with their
offices. 
The I'public meeting" exception pennits the use of public
funds to notify the official's 
constituents of :neetings in the
coruuunity aireCtly related to the officer's 
incumbent governmental
duties .
you have asked rrho erill be considered the t'constituentsri of
an assemblymernber for purposes of this exception during the period
between the adoption of a reapportionment plan and the Novenber
1992 elections.5 The terE ttconstituentsti Lras not defined in
Regulation 18901. we have advised in the past that I'constituentsl
n o.,y such announcement rnay not include the elected officer,s
photograph or signature and nay include only a single uention of
the elected officer's name, except as otherwise perruitted by the
regulation. (Regulation 18901(b) (9) (B) . )
c- As you correctly point out in your letter requesting
assistance, the other exceptions to the roass nailing ristrictions
of the Act are not contingent on the audience being linited to the
off icer,s constituents.


means the persons residing or doinq business in the elected
officer's iistrict. 
(connellv Advice Letter, No. r-9o-524')
Additionally, webster's NeL, world Dictionary (1988) defines
Iconstituentr as rta person who appoints another to act as agent or
representative. " Bllck's Lav Dictionary (1979) defines
t,ctnstituenttr as one "who gives authority to another to act for
hirn...It is also used in the language of politics as a correlative
i- '..p."""ntative, 
' the constituents of a legislator,being those
whom hL represents and whose interests he is to care for in public
affairs; u-ua11y the electors of his district. "
Review of the pertinent law seens to suggest alternate
interpretations. 
F-or example, in situations where a l-egislator's
district 
has been redistrilted but the legistator leaves office
pii"i 
t" the next election, the voters that initially 
elected the
i"girf"t"t 
rrould vote to fill 
the legislator's seat in the special
ef6ction. 
The new district 
would be applicable only after the
n-xt general election. 
(Sloan v. Donoqhue (L942) 20 C'2d 607;
Leoislature v. Reinecke (1973) 10 c'3d 396.)
Conversely, in our telephone conversation of January 30,
1992, you poinied out that Elections code Section 1o212, which
pr""lalr ri:Ies for deternining who wj.II be able to clain the title
i'incumbent" on the election ball'ot, may provide guidance with
respect to the interpretation of Section 89OOl'. In Friends of
Ass'emblvwoman La Follette v- SuDerior court (1982) 134 caI'App'3d
832, the court interPreted the section as follows:
The construction we place on the statute means
that each resident in California witl continue to
be serviced, until the voters nake a choice in the
1982 General EJ.ection, by the representative in the
State Assenbly who uas selected at the 1980 General
Election by voters in the district 
as it was
defined. In addition, residents in geographic
territory which has been shifted to a new and
different assembly district 
may be serviced by the
representative who qualifies as_an incurnbent under
Elictions code section 10212. [6]
Election Code Section 10212 and interpretive case Ia!, does
not control the interpretation of the provisions of the Act.

the needs of residents, being of service to residents in
connection with problems vith State government, providing

interest, coordinating school tours of Sacramento and the capitol
and providing residents with infornation on consuner issues,
landLord-tenant infornation, wonens, rights, auto repair and

Section 81oo3 states that t'this title 
should be liberalLy
construed to accomplish its Purposes.'r (In re Barnhart (1980) 105
cal.App.3A 264.) Section 81013 provides: "Nothing in thj's title
prevents the Legislature or any other state or Iocal agency froh
irnposing additional requirernents on any person if the requirements
do not prevent the person from complying with this tit1e. 
If any
act of Lhe Legislature conflicts with the provisions of this
titJ"e, this title shall prevail- "
Holrever, since the purPose of the exception in Requfation
18901(b) (9) (A) (1) is to perrnit an elected officer to send rneeting
notices'to persons that the officer represents, we bel-ieve that
the case is analogous to the question presented here.
consequently, we lonclude that the Persons residing or doing
busin6ss in- itre elected officer,s existing district 
continue to be
the officer's constituents for purPoses of the exception in
Regulation 18901(b) (9) (A) (1) until the next election.

uaditiorr, to avoid'uncertainty in the service of the poPulation of

territory ghich has been shifted to the district 
for $rhon the
assenblyrnernber qua),ifies as an incurnbent under Elections code
Section 10212 are also the assemblynenber's constituents.
